coredata.dc:title: Efficient light-emitting diodes from emitters of TaSe2 quantum dots and pure carbon dots and carrier injection tailoring of TaSe2
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Yijin
Zeng, Liya
Liu, Yuwei
Yi, Zichuan
Chi, Feng
Liu, Liming
Zhang, Xiaowen
coredata.dc:description: Solution-processed symbiotic TaSe2 quantum dots and carbon dots (TaSe2-QDs/CDs) and pure CDs have been facilely synthesized via liquid-phase ultrasonic exfoliation and hydrothermal reaction. Symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs and CDs show good film morphology and excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence. With CDs (TaSe2-QDs/CDs) as emitter, efficient light-emitting diodes (LEDs) are assembled, showing maximum luminance of 217 (640) cd/m2, external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 0.11 % (0.21 %), luminous efficiency of 0.135 (0.262) cd/A, and power efficiency of 0.045 (0.095) lm/W. Resonance energy transfer from CDs to TaSe2-QDs contributes to the enhanced performance in TaSe2-QDs/CDs based LED. With 2-(4-biphenyl)-5-(4-tert-butylphenyl)-1,3,4-oxadiazole as emitters, normal and inverted UV organic LEDs (OLEDs) are realized using TaSe2-QDs/CDs tailoring hole and electron injection, respectively. The normal (inverted) UV OLEDs show maximum radiance of 1.14 (1.3) mW/cm2, EQE of 0.89 % (0.37 %), and electroluminescence peak of 397 (395) nm. Impedance spectroscopy and current versus voltage characteristics are performed to clarify some mechanisms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
TaSe2 quantum dots
Light-emitting diode
Organic light-emitting diode
Carrier injection tailoring

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs and pure CDs
LEDs with emitters of symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs and pure CDs
Normal UV OLEDs with TaSe2 as HIL
Inverted UV OLEDs with TaSe2 as EIL

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 62065004 , 62075035 ), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation ( 2021B1515420001 ), The Engineering Technology Center of Regular Universities in Guangdong Province ( 2021GCZX005 ), The Innovation Team of Regular Universities in Guangdong Province ( 2021KCXTD040 ), and The Key Laboratory of Regular Universities in Guangdong Province ( 2023KSYS011 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs, and the corresponding HR-TEM images of (b) TaSe2-QDs and (c) CDs.
AFM images of (a) bare ITO, (b) ITO/[TaSe2-QDs/CDs] (0.8 mg/ml), (c) ITO/[TaSe2-QDs/CDs] (0.8 mg/ml) with 8 min UV-O3 treatment, (d) ITO/PVK/[TaSe2-QDs/CDs], and (e) ITO/PVK/CDs.

PL spectra of (a) pure CDs and (b) symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs under different excitation wavelengths. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of TaSe2-QDs/CDs and CDs solutions. (d) TR-PL measurements of TaSe2-QDs/CDs and CDs.

(a) J-V, (b) L-V, (c) EQE characteristics, and (d) luminous/power efficiencies as a function of current density curves for Devices A1 (symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs as emitter) and A2 (pure CDs as emitter).
(a) EL spectra and (b) 1931 CIE color coordinates of Device A1 with symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs as emitter. (c) EL spectra and (d) 1931 CIE color coordinates of Device A2 with pure CDs as emitter.
(a) J-V, (b) R–V, (c) EQE, and (d) EL spectra of the normal UV OLEDs with HILs of TaSe2 having different concentrations of 1, 0.8, 0.5 and 0.2 mg/ml.
(a) J-V, (b) R–V, (c) EQE and (d) EL spectra of the inverted UV OLEDs with EILs of TaSe2 having different concentrations of 0.7, 0.5, 0.3 and 0.1 mg/ml.
Summary of some key parameters of LEDs with symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs and pure CDs as emitters. Some reported LEDs using pure CDs as emitters are also incorporated for comparison.
Performance comparison of the normal UV OLEDs with HILs of TaSe2 having different concentrations of 1, 0.8, 0.5 and 0.2 mg/ml.
Performance comparison of inverted UV OLEDs with EILs of TaSe2 having different concentrations of 0.7, 0.5, 0.3 and 0.1 mg/ml.

head.ce:title: Efficient light-emitting diodes from emitters of TaSe2 quantum dots and pure carbon dots and carrier injection tailoring of TaSe2

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Information Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Electronic Technology, Guilin, 541004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory of Information Materials
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhongshan Branch of State Key Laboratory of Electronic Thin Films and Integrated Devices, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Zhongshan Institute, Zhongshan, 528402, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhongshan Branch of State Key Laboratory of Electronic Thin Films and Integrated Devices
University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhongshan Branch of State Key Laboratory of Electronic Thin Films and Integrated Devices, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Zhongshan Institute, Zhongshan 528402, P.R. China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. 1 Jinji Road, Guilin, Guangxi Province, PR China.
Corresponding author. 1 Xueyuan Road, Zhongshan, Guangdong Province, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solution-processed symbiotic TaSe2 quantum dots and carbon dots (TaSe2-QDs/CDs) and pure CDs have been facilely synthesized via liquid-phase ultrasonic exfoliation and hydrothermal reaction. Symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs and CDs show good film morphology and excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence. With CDs (TaSe2-QDs/CDs) as emitter, efficient light-emitting diodes (LEDs) are assembled, showing maximum luminance of 217 (640) cd/m2, external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 0.11 % (0.21 %), luminous efficiency of 0.135 (0.262) cd/A, and power efficiency of 0.045 (0.095) lm/W. Resonance energy transfer from CDs to TaSe2-QDs contributes to the enhanced performance in TaSe2-QDs/CDs based LED. With 2-(4-biphenyl)-5-(4-tert-butylphenyl)-1,3,4-oxadiazole as emitters, normal and inverted UV organic LEDs (OLEDs) are realized using TaSe2-QDs/CDs tailoring hole and electron injection, respectively. The normal (inverted) UV OLEDs show maximum radiance of 1.14 (1.3) mW/cm2, EQE of 0.89 % (0.37 %), and electroluminescence peak of 397 (395) nm. Impedance spectroscopy and current versus voltage characteristics are performed to clarify some mechanisms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Newly emerged semiconductor nanomaterials of quantum dots (QDs) have been highly spotlighted due to their quantum confinement effects, surface effects and edge effects, showing tunable luminescence spectrum, narrow full width at half maximum (FWHM), good color purity and high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) [1–3]. The QDs based light-emitting diodes (QLEDs) almost perfectly meet the requirement of next-generation displays and lighting. However, most high-performance QLEDs contain toxic elements of Cd and Pb, which is contrary to the environment-friendly concept and counteracts commercial applications [4]. Carbon dots (CDs) as a new type of zero-dimensional QDs possess abundant carbon raw materials, non-toxicity, and good water solubility. CDs can also be treated with low-cost solution process, which caters for scalable manufacturing and sustainable development [5–7]. The PL characteristics of CDs have been intensively investigated covering emission from UV to near-infrared [8,9]. The electroluminescence (EL) characteristics from CDs have also been received increasing attentions since Ma et al. first reported pure CDs based LEDs with maximum luminance of 35 cd/m2 and luminous efficiency of 0.022 cd/A in 2011 [10]. Yuan et al. fabricated pure CDs based blue LEDs with enhanced luminance of 136 cd/m2 and luminous efficiency of 0.084 cd/A [11]. Tailoring device configuration and carrier injection helps performance promotion and color tunability [12]. Based on localized excitonic luminescence, pure CDs-LEDs show quite low turn-on voltage of 2.4 V, the luminance and luminous efficiency have been reached 60.2 cd/m2 and 0.019 cd/A, respectively [13]. In our groups, pure CDs-LEDs with luminance of 30 cd/m2, luminous efficiency of 0.089 cd/A, and power efficiency of 0.046 lm/W have been explored [14]. We also developed high-performance pure CDs-LEDs with luminance of 214 cd/m2 and luminous efficiency of 0.31 cd/A, although the EL spectra are seriously affected by adjacent organic layer [15]. Further performance enhancement can be readily realized from CDs-doped emissive layer [16–18].

Transition metal diselenides (TMDs) with non-toxicity, environmentally friendliness, and 2D layered structure have also been extensively investigated [19]. Stronger quantum confinement and edge effects of TMDs result in better optical/electrical properties and wider applications in comparison with other conventional nanosheets [20–23]. TaSe2, as a member of TMDs, exhibits promising prospects in lasers [24], photodetectors [25], and all-optical switches [26]. However, there are rare reports on TaSe2 applications in LEDs. TaSe2 QDs as luminescent materials are of great significance for broadening applications as well as advancing healthy LEDs. In addition, TaSe2 with suitable surface work function could be used as interfacial layer in organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs). Herein, we facilely synthesized TaSe2 QDs and pure CDs with a purpose of developing novel healthy LEDs. The synthesized TaSe2 denotes as symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs, since the TaSe2 QDs contains CDs during synthesis process. LEDs with emitters of pure CDs and symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs are investigated in details. The pure CDs-LEDs show maximum luminance of 217 cd/m2 and luminous efficiency of 0.135 cd/A, which tops state-of-the-art LEDs with pure CDs as emitter. The luminance and luminous efficiency have been enhanced to 640 cd/m2 and 0.262 cd/A for symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs based LEDs. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first reported LEDs with TaSe2-QDs or TaSe2-QDs/CDs as emitters. In the case of TaSe2-QDs/CDs serving as hole injection layer (HIL) or electron injection layer (EIL) in OLEDs, short-wavelength UV OLEDs are selected considering their rather high energy barriers between electrode and organic emitter, which is quite suitable for hole/electron injection investigation. Our researches not only provide some new ideas for pure CDs or symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs based LEDs but also accelerate TMDs applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs and pure CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs solutions were facilely synthesized by using ultrasonic liquid phase exfoliation and hydrothermal method. Firstly, commercially purchased TaSe2 powders (Suzhou Beike Nanotechnology Co., Ltd., 99.9 %) were dispersed in 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), and ultrasonically exfoliated for 24 h. Then, the obtained suspension was transferred into a stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min. Afterwards, the supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter to obtain symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs solutions which were served as emissive layer (EML), HIL, and EIL of LEDs. The pure CDs solutions were also synthesized as symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs without adding TaSe2 powders. Fig. S1 in Supporting Information shows the schematic synthesis process of symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs solutions and serving as HIL, EML and EIL in LEDs as well as some molecular structures of organic materials used.

All the studied LEDs were fabricated on ITO-coated glass which was pretreated with chemical solvents, deionized water and UV-O3 irradiation. Symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs or pure CDs as EML are investigated by assembling multilayer LEDs (Devices A1-A2). Firstly, HIL of poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly (styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) was spin-coated upon ITO at 3000 rpm for 60 s, and annealed at 120 °C for 15 min under ambient conditions. Then, hole-transport layer (HTL) of poly (9-vinylcarbazole) (PVK, 5 mg/ml, chlorobenzene solvent) was spin-coated upon the PEDOT:PSS at 4000 rpm for 60 s, and annealed at 120 °C for 15 min in N2-filled box. EML of TaSe2-QDs/CDs or CDs was spin-coated upon PVK at 2000 rpm for 40 s with annealing at 80 °C for 20 min. Afterwards, the 2,2′,2"-(1,3,5-benzinetriyl)-tris(1-phenyl-1-H-benzimidazole) (TPBi, 100 nm) as electron-transport layer (ETL), LiF (1 nm) as EIL, and Al (120 nm) as reflective cathode were sequentially thermally deposited under a vacuum of 4 × 10−4 Pa. The active area of all the studied LEDs was 5 mm × 5 mm.
Device A1: ITO/PEDOT:PSS/PVK(5 mg/ml)/[TaSe2-QDs/CDs]/TPBi (100 nm)/LiF (1 nm)/Al(120 nm).
Device A2: ITO/PEDOT:PSS/PVK/CDs/TPBi/LiF/Al.

The normal UV OLEDs (Devices C1–C5) were assembled with TaSe2 as HIL, which was spin-coated from different concentrations of symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs solution at 3000 rpm with ambient annealing at 230 °C for 20 min and UV-O3 treatment for 8 min. Afterwards, 4,4′-bis (carbazol-9-yl) biphenyl (CBP, 25 nm) as HTL, 2-(4-biphenyl)-5-(4-tert-butylphenyl)-1,3,4-oxadiazole (PBD, 30 nm) as EML for its satisfactory fluorescence, appropriate highest occupied molecular orbital (∼6.1 eV) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (∼2.4 eV) levels, 4,7-diphenyl-1,10-phenanthroline (BPhen, 150 nm) as ETL, LiF (2 nm) as EIL, and Al (120 nm) as reflective cathode were vacuum thermally deposited.
Device C1: ITO/TaSe2 (1 mg/ml)/CBP (25 nm)/PBD (30 nm)/BPhen (150 nm)/LiF (2 nm)/Al (120 nm).
Device C2: ITO/TaSe2 (0.8 mg/ml)/CBP/PBD/BPhen/LiF/Al.
Device C3: ITO/TaSe2 (0.5 mg/ml)/CBP/PBD/BPhen/LiF/Al.
Device C4: ITO/TaSe2 (0.2 mg/ml)/CBP/PBD/BPhen/LiF/Al.
Device C5: ITO/TaSe2 (0.8 mg/ml, without UV-O3 treatment)/CBP/PBD/BPhen/LiF/Al.

Inverted UV OLEDs (Devices D1-D4) using TaSe2 as EIL were also constructed from different concentrations of symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs solution. The fabrication process of EIL is similar to the HIL excluding UV-O3 treatment.
Device D1: ITO/TaSe2 (0.7 mg/ml)/BPhen (25 nm)/PBD (35 nm)/CBP (70 nm)/MoO3 (5 nm)/Al (120 nm).
Device D2: ITO/TaSe2 (0.5 mg/ml)/BPhen/PBD/CBP/MoO3/Al.
Device D3: ITO/TaSe2 (0.3 mg/ml)/BPhen/PBD/CBP/MoO3/Al.
Device D4: ITO/TaSe2 (0.1 mg/ml)/BPhen/PBD/CBP/MoO3/Al.
Here, BPhen, PBD, and CBP were served as ETL, EML, and HTL, respectively. MoO3 and Al were served as HIL and reflective anode.

Microstructure of symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs was characterized with high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEOL JEM F200). Surface morphology of the coated films was characterized with atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker MultiMode 8). Absorption properties of the spin-coated film were measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Lambda 365). PL characteristics were tested by Edinburgh FS5 fluorescence spectrometer. Impedance spectroscopy was tested with an impedance analyzer (Agilent 4294A) in “Z-θ” mode with sweeping frequency of 100 Hz and a.c. signals of 50 mV. Luminance (L), Radiance (R), EL spectra, and Current density versus Voltage (J-V) characteristics were measured with Keithley 2400 source meter, Konica Minolta LS-150 luminance meter, Ocean Optics Maya-2000-pro spectra scan, and data acquisition software.

The TEM images of synthesized symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs are shown in Fig. 1 . TaSe2-QDs and/or CDs are uniformly distributed, showing an average size of about 4 nm (Fig. 1(a)). The corresponding HR-TEM images behave distinct lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 0.22 nm (Fig. 1(b)), which is assigned to the (104) crystal plane of TaSe2 [27]. While the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm shown in Fig. 1(c) corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [14]. The TEM measurements confirm the successful synthesis of TaSe2-QDs and the coexistence of TaSe2-QDs and CDs. It can be reasonably deduced that the CDs are produced by carbonization of NMP during ultrasonic treatment and hydrothermal reaction.

The AFM images with scanning area of 3 μm × 3 μm of symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs films or CDs films coated upon ITO substrate are shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2(a) shows the surface morphology of bare ITO. Large ITO grains and obvious grain boundaries are observed. Some small surface pinholes can also be seen, showing a root mean square (RMS) roughness of 3.37 nm. As shown in Fig. 2(b), TaSe2-QDs/CDs form uniform film morphology on ITO substrate. However, some TaSe2-QDs/CDs agglomerate and form burrs during high temperature annealing, which results in a rough film surface with a slight increased RMS roughness of 4.08 nm. Fortunately, after 8 min UV-O3 treatment, some burrs on the film surface are removed, and some vacancies are filled with oxygen under strong ultraviolet light irradiation and high concentration ozone treatment. The RMS roughness is decreased to 3.12 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(c). The TaSe2-QDs/CDs and CDs also form uniform and flat films on the surface of PVK, showing RMS roughness of 3.39 nm and 1.165 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2(d) and (e). The superior film morphology is of great significance for subsequent fabrication of efficient LEDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectra delineated in Fig. S2 in Supporting Information show that the TaSe2-QDs/CDs films exhibit quite weak light absorption in the range of 350–800 nm. The UV-O3 treatment has negligible effects on the UV–vis absorption. These phenomena indicate that there is weak light loss when the emitted light from LEDs pass through these TaSe2-QDs/CDs films.

The PL spectra of pure CDs and symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs are shown in Fig. 3 (a) and (b), respectively. The PL intensity and emission peak of both CDs and TaSe2-QDs/CDs are strongly dependent of the excitation wavelength. In details, the PL spectra redshift with increasing excitation wavelength. This may be caused by the presence of multiple band gap energies due to the multiple sizes of the synthesized QDs [28–30]. The CDs reach a maximum PL intensity under 325 nm excitation, which produces about 416 nm emission. In contrast, the TaSe2-QDs/CDs reaches a maximum PL intensity under 385 nm excitation, which produces 493 nm emission. Moreover, the PL intensity of TaSe2-QDs/CDs is much higher than that of CDs as a result of resonance energy transfer between TaSe2-QDs and CDs, resulting in the energy transferring from CDs to TaSe2-QDs [31]. Fig. 3(c) shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and TaSe2-QDs/CDs. There are no obvious characteristic peaks for both CDs and TaSe2-QDs/CDs, owing to the quantum confinement and edge effects [32]. The insets in Fig. 3(c) illustrate that TaSe2-QDs/CDs solution and CDs solution behave brown and white, respectively, under natural states, and emit yellow-green and blue-green light, respectively, under 395 nm excitation. Fig. 3(d) shows the measured fluorescence lifetime of TaSe2-QDs/CDs and CDs from time resolved (TR) PL characteristics. It is estimated to be 8.1 ns for TaSe2-QDs/CDs, and 4.2 ns for CDs. The prolongated fluorescence lifetime of TaSe2-QDs/CDs benefits for light emission, which creates more conditions for the development of efficient and stable LEDs using symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LEDs with emitters of symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs and pure CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The electro-optical conversion properties of Device A1 with symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs as EML and Device A2 with pure CDs as EML are comparatively delineated in Fig. 4 . Some key parameters are listed in Table 1 . Device A1 shows maximum luminance of 640 cd/m2@11 V, external quantum efficiency (EQE) of 0.21 %@162.8 mA/cm2, luminous efficiency of 0.262 cd/A, and power efficiency of 0.095 lm/W. In comparison, Device A2 shows maximum luminance of 217 cd/m2@12V, EQE of 0.11 %@123.3 mA/cm2, luminous efficiency of 0.135 cd/A, and power efficiency of 0.045 lm/W. The electro-optical properties of Device A1 are superior to Device A2, owing to the resonance energy transfer between TaSe2-QDs and CDs [31]. Some state-of-the-art reported LEDs with pure CDs as emitters are also incorporated in Table 1. Our synthesized pure CDs deriving from NMP as precursor behave overwhelmingly superiority over other methods in constructing pure CDs-LEDs. The symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs provides a novel approach for assembling efficient LEDs.
With in-depth understanding the EL properties of TaSe2-QDs/CDs and CDs, Device B1 with PVK as EML and Device B2 with TPBi as EML are fabricated as reference.
Device B1: ITO/PEDOT:PSS/PVK (5 mg/ml)/LiF (1 nm)/Al.
Device B2: ITO/PEDOT:PSS/PVK (5 mg/ml)/TPBi (100 nm)/LiF (1 nm)/Al.

As shown in the EL spectrum of Device B1 (Fig. S3a in Supporting Information), PVK shows three emission peaks of 421, 499 and 600 nm. The 421 nm is assigned to the fluorescence emission of PVK, the 499 nm is considered as the phosphorescence emission of PVK, and the 600 nm is resulted from the dielectric emission of PVK [33,34]. The emission peaks of PVK are negligibly shifted under different voltages. The EL spectra of Device B2 (Fig. S3b in Supporting Information) show that TPBi behaves only one emission peak at 376 nm which is also independent of driving voltages.

The EL spectra and 1931 Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) color coordinates of Device A1 (symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs as emitter) at different voltages are shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b). At 10 V, the EL spectrum of Device A1 shows an emission peak at 424 nm from the fluorescence emission of PVK, and an emission shoulder at 485 nm from the phosphorescence emission of PVK and TaSe2-QDs/CDs emission. The corresponding CIE color coordinates are (0.2969,0.3114). At 11 V, the EL spectrum shows similar shape with enhanced EL intensity and CIE color coordinates of (0.2974,0.3116). The device gives yellow-green emission at 12 V, showing an EL peak at 562 nm predominantly resulted from TaSe2-QDs/CDs. The PVK emission is considerably weakened, and the CIE color coordinates shift to (0.3636,0.3970). At 13 V, the EL from TaSe2-QDs/CDs is absolutely dominated, showing CIE color coordinates of (0.3810,0.4179).

Fig. 5(c) and (d) show the EL spectra and CIE color coordinates of Device A2 (pure CDs as emitter). The EL spectra consist of the fluorescence emission of PVK and small amounts of CDs emission at low voltages of 11 V and 12 V. The corresponding CIE color coordinates are (0.2709, 0.2611) and (0.2885, 0.2933), respectively. At 13 V, the device shows white emission with CIE color coordinates of (0.3092,0.3285). The corresponding EL spectrum originates from the complex emission of CDs and the phosphorescence emission of PVK with an emission peak of 505 nm. Our results pave an alternative approach for developing pure CDs based healthy white LEDs. At 14 V, the device emits yellow-green light with CIE color coordinates of (0.3330,0.3645). The EL peak locates at 529 nm predominantly comes from the CDs emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Normal UV OLEDs with TaSe2 as HIL
ce:section.ce:para: The J-R-V, EQE and EL spectra of normal NUV OLEDs with TaSe2 as HIL spin-coated from different concentrations of symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs solution are shown in Fig. 6 . Some key parameters are listed in Table 2 . The electro-optical performance of Device C2 (0.8 mg/ml TaSe2-QDs/CDs) tops the others, showing maximum radiance of 1.14 mW/cm2 at 13.5 V, and maximum EQE of 0.89 % at 2.9 mA/cm2. The normal OLED gives typical UV emission from PBD emitter, showing an EL peak of 397 nm and FWHM of 62 nm. In Comparison with Devices C1, C3 and C4, the maximum EQE of Device C2 has been increased by 0.56 %, 61.8 % and 61.8 %, respectively. This indicates that altering the concentration of TaSe2-QDs/CDs solution paves a way for tuning hole injection and thus optimizing carrier balance in the EML as well as promoting device performance. It is noticed that Device C5 shows rather low efficiency and radiance, which indicates that UV-O3 treatment plays an important role in improving hole injection ability of TaSe2.
The hole injection ability of TaSe2 is further investigated by constructing the following hole-only cells (HOCs, Cells H1–H3) and implementing impedance spectroscopy and I–V analysis.
Cell H1: ITO/TaSe2 (1 mg/ml)/CBP/Al.
Cell H2: ITO/TaSe2 (0.8 mg/ml)/CBP/Al.
Cell H3: ITO/TaSe2 (0.5 mg/ml)/CBP/Al.

The curves of impedance, phase and current versus voltage characteristics (Z-V, φ-V and I–V) of the HOCs are shown in Fig. S4 in Supporting Information. At low voltages, the HOCs exhibit high impedance with magnitude of about 105 Ω and a negative phase angle of about −90°. As the voltage increases, the impedance gradually decreases, and the phase angle changes to 0°. This process suggests that HOCs transit from an insulating state to a semiconducting state with increasing voltage [35]. Such a transition voltage reflects the hole injection capacity. Cell H2 (0.8 mg/ml TaSe2 as HIL) behaves lower transition voltage, manifesting stronger hole injection. It can also be seen from the I–V curves that Cell H2 shows higher current at the same voltage, indicating Cell H2 behaves superior hole injection capability and contributes to the promotion of device performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inverted UV OLEDs with TaSe2 as EIL
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 and Table 3 show the electro-optical performance of inverted UV OLEDs with TaSe2 as EIL spin-coated from different concentrations of symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs solution. The EQE as a function of current density charateristics of Devices D1-D4 show a trend of increasing at low current densities and then gradually decreasing after reaching a maximum. Among them, Device D3 (0.3 mg/ml TaSe2-QDs/CDs) achieves a maximum EQE of 0.37 % at 69.5 mA/cm2, which is increased by 32.1 %, 2.7 % and 12.1 % in comparison with Device D1, D2 and D4, respectively. Devices D2 and D3 give maximum radiance of 1.3 mW/cm2 at 14 V and 1.06 mW/cm2 at 16 V, respectively, producing UV emission from PBD with EL peaks of 395–398 nm and FWMHs of 55–56 nm. This indicates that an appropriate TaSe2 concentration promotes electron injection and accordingly enhancing the performance of inverted UV OLEDs.
Electron injection characteristics of TaSe2 as EIL are also investigated by configurating electron-only cells (EOCs, Cells E1-E3).
Cell E1: ITO/TaSe2 (0.1 mg/ml)/BPhen/MoO3/Al.
Cell E2: ITO/TaSe2 (0.3 mg/ml)/BPhen/MoO3/Al.
Cell E3: ITO/TaSe2 (0.5 mg/ml)/BPhen/MoO3/Al.

The Z-V, φ-V and I–V curves shown Fig. S5 in Supporting Information indicate that Cell E2 (0.3 mg/ml TaSe2 as EIL) behaves the lowest transition voltage and the highest current, indicating the most efficient electron injection and accordingly contributing to device performance enhancement.

Symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs and pure CDs have been facilely synthesized via liquid-phase ultrasonic exfoliation and hydrothermal reaction. TEM measurements confirm that the synthesized TaSe2-QDs contain CDs. Both TaSe2-QDs/CDs and pure CDs show good film morphology and excitation wavelength dependent PL emission characteristics. Efficient LEDs with TaSe2-QDs/CDs and pure CDs as EMLs, TaSe2-QDs/CDs as HILs or EILs are investigated in details. The LED with pure CDs as EML shows maximum luminance of 217 cd/m2, EQE of 0.11 %, luminous efficiency of 0.135 cd/A, and power efficiency of 0.045 lm/W. Further enhanced performance with maximum luminance of 640 cd/m2, EQE of 0.21 %, luminous efficiency of 0.262 cd/A, and power efficiency of 0.095 lm/W are achieved in the LED with symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs as EML due to the resonance energy transfer from CDs to TaSe2-QDs. With PBD as EML, efficient normal and inverted UV OLEDs are assembled using symbiotic TaSe2-QDs/CDs as HIL and EIL, respectively. The normal (inverted) UV OLEDs show maximum radiance of 1.14 (1.3) mW/cm2, EQE of 0.89 % (0.37 %), EL peak of 397 (395) nm with TaSe2-QDs/CDs tailoring hole (electron) injection. Impedance spectroscopy and I–V curves analysis indicates that appropriate concentration of TaSe2-QDs/CDs extremely tunes hole and electron injection and accordingly contributing to LEDs performance promotion.

Yijin Tang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Liya Zeng: Conceptualization, Methodology. Yuwei Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology. Zichuan Yi: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Feng Chi: Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Liming Liu: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Xiaowen Zhang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (62065004, 62075035), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2021B1515420001), The Engineering Technology Center of Regular Universities in Guangdong Province (2021GCZX005), The Innovation Team of Regular Universities in Guangdong Province (2021KCXTD040), and The Key Laboratory of Regular Universities in Guangdong Province (2023KSYS011).
